content.full_text:
State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 · SACRAMENTO, 95804 · 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

July 27, 1977

Mr. Robert E. LaNove
Assistant Commissioner
Office of Policy
Department of Corporations
1025 P Street
Sacramento, CA 95814

Re: Opinion Request
    No. 77-019

Dear Mr. LaNove:

Thank you for your memorandum of June 27, 1977,
requesting the Commission to issue an opinion concerning
potential disqualification and reporting obligations of
designated employees of the Department of Corporations pur-
suant to Government Code Section 87100 and the Department's
Conflict of Interest Code. Because the request does not
present substantial questions of interpretation, the Commission will not issue a formal opinion. See 2 Cal. Adm. Code
Section 18329. However, I trust the following informal
advice will resolve the questions.

You state in your letter that the Department has
pending before it an application by the Bank of America to
issue mortgage pool certificates. In reviewing the application, the Department must decide whether mortgage pool certificates may be sold as securities under the California Securities
Laws. This question is one of first impression for the
Department and its resolution will set a standard for treatment
of similar applications in the future. Certain employees
specified in your memorandum have a financial relationship
with the Bank of America in that they hold a Bankamericard
or a VISA credit card, have a checking or savings account
with the bank, or control a bank account of an organization
of which they are a member. In addition, Bank of America's
application states that it intends to employ Wells Fargo
Bank as a trustee in this transaction. Two of the named
employees have an existing loan from Wells Fargo Bank and
one is currently negotiating a loan. Another of the individuals has a personal bank account with Wells Fargo.

TECHNICAL ASSISTANCE ADMINISTRATION (916) 322-5660 · EXECUTIVE LEGAL 322-5901 · ENFORCEMENT CONFLICT OF INTEREST 322-6441

Mr. Robert E. LaNoue
July 27, 1977
Page Two

You ask, first, whether the individuals named in
your letter must disqualify themselves from the review of
this application. It is our conclusion that no disqualifi-
cation is required.

Government Code Sections 87100 and 87103 of the
Political Reform Act require that an official disqualify
himself from making, participating in the making of, in any
way attempting to use his official position to influence a
governmental decision "if it is reasonably foreseeable that
the decision will have a material financial effect, distin-
guishable from its effect on the public generally, on ...
[a]ny source of income, other than loans by a commercial
lending institution in the regular course of business, ag-
gregating two hundred fifty dollars ($250) or more in value
received by or promised to the public official within twelve
months prior to the time the decision is made ...." (Section
87103) (emphasis added). Thus, one of the factors required
to be present in this case before disqualification is triggered
is that Bank of America or Wells Fargo Bank be a source of
income to the employees involved.

"Income" is defined broadly in Section 82030 of
the Act to mean "income of any nature from any source,"
including, among other things, any "advance, payment, ...
interest." "Payment," in turn, is defined as "a payment,
distribution, transfer, loan, advance, deposit, gift or
other rendering of money, property, services or anything
else of value, whether tangible or intangible" (Section
82044). Excluded from the definition of income is "[i]nterest,
dividends or premiums on a time or demand deposit in a financial
institution" (Section 82030(b)(7)).

Applying those definitions to the situation you
pose, it is our conclusion that an individual does not receive
income simply by maintaining a checking or savings account
at a bank. Funds deposited in a bank remain the property of
the depositor and their withdrawal cannot fairly be said to
be a payment to the holder of the account. Moreover, any
interest the depositer receives is specifically exempted
from the definition of income by Section 82030(b)(7).

We also do not believe that the use of a bank
credit card ordinarily creates reportable income. While a
literal reading of the Act's definitions of "income" and
"payment" may be said to cover the advancing of credit, we
have concluded that the policy objectives of the Act are not

Mr. Robert E. LaNoue
July 27, 1977
Page Three


served by such broad reporting. We would require a person
to report the receipt of credit as income only if the credit
is extended on special terms not available to similarly
situated members of the general public. We assume that your
employees are being extended credit on the same basis as all
other holders of Bankamericard and VISA cards, and therefore
conclude that they are not receiving income within the meaning
of the Act.

Your employees who have loans outstanding with
Wells Fargo Bank do have a reporting obligation. However,
the existence of these loans do not trigger disqualification
because of the exception set forth in Section 87103, which
we quoted above.

Your second question relates to whether the individ-
uals named in your letter must report any of the following
types of transactions with Bank of America or Wells Fargo
Bank:

1. Purchase of goods or services with a bank
credit card when the outstanding balance is paid
within 30 days;

2. Purchase of goods or services with a bank
credit card when the outstanding balance is not
paid within 30 days, thereby causing interest to
accrue;

3. Withdrawal of funds from a bank account; and

4. Writing of a check on a bank account.

As explained above, a reporting obligation might
arise under the first two examples, but only if special
credit is extended outside the ordinary course of business.
The third and fourth examples create no reporting obligation.

I hope this advice answers your questions. If you
wish to appeal the denial of your opinion request, you may
do so pursuant to 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18321. If you
have any questions, please contact Kenneth Goshorn, an attorney
in the Commission's Conflict of Interest Division.

Sincerely,

Michael Bennett
Executive Director

MB:plh

Request No.        4.20-77
                                    Date Received_______
                                    5-4-77
                                    Response Due________


PATRICK J. SAMPSON
City Attorney
                                    THE CITY OF POMONA
                                    Box 660, Pomona, CA 91769  (714) 620-2071
                                    Office of the City Attorney

                                    APRIL 13, 1977


Hon. Daniel H. Lowenstein, Chairman
and Members
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
P. O. Box 607
Sacramento, California    95804

RE:  REQUEST FOR FORMAL OPINION

Gentlemen:

The Community Redevelopment Commission of the City of Pomona
has requested me to ask for a formal opinion as to whether
that Commission must disclose as a "designated employee" under
the Conflict of Interest Code currently in preparation by my
office for city agencies.

Heretofore, I have requested of your staff an informal opinion,
a copy of which is attached hereto.  It was, and is my opinion
that this Commission, formed under Section 33201, Health and
Safety Code, should not be required to disclose as it makes no
final governmental decision.  The scope of its duties is mainly
advisory to the City Council of Pomona, which also sits as the
Governing Body of the Redevelopment Agency of the City of
Pomona.  Its recommendations are not "rubber stamped" by the
Governing Body, but on many occasions are rejected or modified.

The closest that the Commission comes to making a final govern-
mental decision is when it prepares a redevelopment plan for a
project area, prior to its creation.  The final authority whether
to adopt the plan and the project area rests with the City Council,
and no Commission action relating to redevelopment can be made
without the prior final approval of the governing body or the
City Council.  (see §33365, Health and Safety Code)

I believe that a formal opinion is necessary as this Commission
is created by State law, so presumably there may be many such bodies
throughout the State.

Hon. Daniel H. Lowenstein, Chairman
and Members
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
April 13, 1977
Page 2


I, therefore, request your Commission to draft a formal opinion
regarding this matter. Please contact me if you desire a
further factual exposition.

Respectfully submitted,

THE CITY OF POMONA

By [signature]
PATRICK J. SAMPSON
City Attorney

PJS:tb

Attachment

cc: Jerrold R. Gonce,
City Administrator

Charles Stillinger, Chairman
Community Redevelopment Commission

Alfredo Flores, Research Specialist
Conflicts of Interest Division, F.P.P.C.

sections.question: 1) Whether certain designated employees at the Department of Corporations with financial relationships to Bank of America and Wells Fargo Bank must disqualify themselves from reviewing a Bank of America application to issue mortgage pool Certificates; 2) Whether such employees must report various transactions with these banks (credit card purchases, account withdrawals, check writing, etc.)
sections.conclusion: No disqualification is required. Maintaining checking/savings accounts and using standard credit cards do not constitute reportable income. Employees with loans from Wells Fargo Bank do have a reporting obligation, but this does not trigger disqualification under the exception in Section 87103. Routine banking transactions (account withdrawals, check writing, and standard credit card purchases) create no reporting obligation; special credit terms outside ordinary business practice would trigger reporting requirements.
sections.question_synthetic: The Department of Corporations asked whether employees with existing financial relationships to Bank of America and Wells Fargo Bank must disqualify themselves from reviewing a Bank of America mortgage pool certificate application, and whether they must report routine banking transactions.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC concluded that no disqualification was required based on standard banking relationships, though employees with outstanding loans must report them. Routine banking transactions and standard credit card use do not create reporting obligations under the Political Reform Act.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document contains OCR errors affecting readability in places. Primary advice letter regarding Opinion Request No. 77-019 is clearly identifiable. Document also includes an unrelated incoming request letter from City of Pomona dated April 18, 1977 requesting formal opinion on Community Redevelopment Commission disclosure requirements; only the first letter (77-019) appears to be the main FPPC response document described in the parsing notes.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: 1) Whether certain designated employees at the Department of Corporations with financial relationships to Bank of America and Wells Fargo Bank must disqualify themselves from reviewing a Bank of America application to issue mortgage pool Certificates; 2) Whether such employees must report various transactions with these banks (credit card purchases, account withdrawals, check writing, etc.)

CONCLUSION: No disqualification is required. Maintaining checking/savings accounts and using standard credit cards do not constitute reportable income. Employees with loans from Wells Fargo Bank do have a reporting obligation, but this does not trigger disqualification under the exception in Section 87103. Routine banking transactions (account withdrawals, check writing, and standard credit card purchases) create no reporting obligation; special credit terms outside ordinary business practice would trigger reporting requirements.

embedding.summary: FPPC advice letter responding to Department of Corporations' inquiry regarding conflict of interest disqualification and reporting obligations for employees reviewing a Bank of America application where those employees have financial relationships with the banks involved. The Commission concluded no disqualification was needed and provided guidance on what constitutes reportable income and transactions.
